fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ANDERSON J:

INTRODUCTION

The applicant de facto husband (“the husband”) brought proceedings against the respondent de facto wife (“the wife”) by way of an Initiating Application filed on 11 December 2023. By that application, the husband invited the Court to make orders adjusting the parties’ property interests pursuant to s 90SM of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). This application followed the breakdown of the parties’ relationship on 28 January 2023.
On the same day, the husband commenced parenting proceedings in relation to the female child of the relationship. She is now aged seven (7) years.
On 27 October 2025, I made final parenting orders on an undefended basis. I did so in circumstances where the mother without explanation failed to attend at the final hearing. Subsequently, and on 3 December 2025, I made orders adjusting the parties’ property interests. My reasons for judgment are recorded in Arrigucci & Franjic (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 877.
These reasons relate to:
(a) The husband’s Application in a Proceeding filed on 2 January 2026. By that application, the husband seeks an order for the costs of and incidental to the proceedings on an indemnity basis or alternatively, the husband’s costs of the proceedings as assessed; and

(b) The paternal grandmother’s Amended Application in a Proceeding filed on 15 January 2026. By that application, the paternal grandmother seeks an order that the wife meet her costs of and incidental to:

(i) The hearing, which occurred on 16 June 2025;

(ii) Costs for “five full days of hearing”; and

(iii) Costs for the “preparation and whole costs associated with the Costs Application and Hearing”.

I have determined that it is appropriate to make an order for costs in favour of each of the husband and the paternal grandmother.

BACKGROUND

The husband is aged forty-three (43) years.[1] He works as a professional manager.[2]
The wife is aged forty-four (44) years.[3] She is unemployed.
On 11 December 2023, the husband commenced parenting proceedings. On 2 May 2025, the husband also made an application in relation to the financial dispute between the parties.
The competing parenting applications concerned a female child who is now aged seven years. She is the only child of the parties.[4] In Arrigucci & Franjic (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 877, I record that the child suffers from anxiety disorder, Global Developmental Delay and deficits in social communication and social interaction.
On 4 April 2024, the husband and the wife appeared before a Judicial Registrar for the first time albeit between 8 February 2024 and that date, a Judicial Registrar made five Orders of a procedural nature in Chambers. With the consent of the parties and the Independent Children’s Lawyer, the Judicial Registrar made an order requiring each of the parties to undertake a hair follicle drug test and alcohol test in accordance with Australian Standards. The mother did not comply with the Judicial Registrar’s order despite advising an expert engaged by the parties that “she does not drink much alcohol” and “she does not take drugs”.[5]
On 6 June 2024, a Judicial Registrar made orders for the preparation of a Family Report by an expert appointed by the parties pursuant to the terms of Chapter 7 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (“the Rules”). It was a term of the order that the parties authorise the withdrawal of a sum of $7,400 from the mortgage redraw account secured over the parties’ property at Suburb C in the State of New South Wales and that the husband otherwise be responsible for paying the remaining sum of $4,100.
On 13 December 2024, and following the parties’ participation in a mediation, the Court made an order that the child live with the wife and otherwise spend supervised time with the husband as follows:
(a) On 26 December 2024 and 2 January 2025 such time to be supervised by the paternal grandparents (or either of them) in a public location;

(b) Thereafter, on each alternate Saturday at a Contact Service; and

(c) As and from 7 March 2025, on each intervening Friday from the conclusion of school to 6.00pm with such time to be supervised by the paternal grandparents (or either of them).

Orders for supervised time between the child and the husband were made in circumstances where the wife’s youngest child from a previous relationship asserted to the wife that the husband “molested her when she was 8 years old”.[6] The husband was subsequently charged with indecently assaulting a child under the age of sixteen years. In May or June 2024, and following a trial at the Magistrates Court, the husband was found not guilty of the charges against him.[7]
On 26 March 2025, the applications were listed before Justice Riethmuller for a Case Management Hearing. The wife failed to attend. The hearing was adjourned to 7 April 2025 at which time the wife again failed to attend. This being so, Justice Riethmuller:
(a) Adjourned the applications to 2 May 2025; and

(b) Made an order pursuant to r 11.68(b) of the Rules for a warrant to issue for the wife’s arrest such warrant to lie in the Registry until the adjourned date.

On 2 May 2025, the competing applications were listed before me. The Court file reflects that the competing applications were so listed in circumstances where on 28 April 2025, the paternal grandmother filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking leave to join the proceedings. The Notations to my Orders were in the following terms:
The competing parenting applications were listed at 9.30 am today. The Judicial Associate to the Honourable Justice Anderson advised the Court that having called the mother three times in the Court precinct, the mother failed to appear. Court security has also called the matter via the public address system.
The mother was served personally with the Orders made by the Honourable Justice Riethmuller on 29 March 2025 and the affidavit of [Ms F] filed on 8 April 2025 refers.
The mother failed to attend at the hearing before the Honourable Justice Riethmuller listed on 26 March 2025 and 7 April 2025.
I am advised by the father's solicitor, and this is confirmed by the Judicial Associate to the Honourable Justice Riethmuller, that although the mother failed to attend at the hearing listed at 9.30 am on 7 April 2025, the matter was recalled at 12.39 pm that same day in circumstances where the mother ultimately attended at the Court. At that time, the mother was advised of the necessity to attend today’s hearing and that if she did not do so, a warrant would be issued for her arrest.
The matter was mentioned at 9.30 am today. The mother failed to appear. At some juncture after 10.00 am, the mother contacted the Chambers of the Honourable Justice Anderson by email and indicated that her motor vehicle had broken down and had to be towed. This being so, the mother says that she is unable to attend at the hearing today.
F. The mother appears via Microsoft Teams today.
The mother has advised the Court this day that she will comply with the terms of Order 2(c) of the Orders made on 30 December 2024, namely, that she will make the child available for collection from [L School] at 3.00 pm today, with the father returning the child to the mother at 6.00 pm with such time to be supervised on a without admissions basis by either or both of the paternal grandparents.
On 28 April 2025 the paternal grandmother filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking an order for her joinder in these proceedings.
The agent for the Independent Children’s Lawyer has advised the Court that the mother facilitated the child’s attendance at her office on Tuesday 29 April 2025.
The father and the Independent Children’s Lawyer seek an urgent interim hearing date in circumstances where they assert that the child is at an unacceptable risk of harm in the care of the mother.
On 2 May 2025 the father filed an Amended Initiating Application seeking to join financial issues to these proceedings.
On 2 May 2025, I directed the wife to file and serve an affidavit by 4.00pm that day deposing to the reasons why she was unable to attend at the hearing listed earlier that day. The wife did not comply with my order.
On 5 May 2025, the matter was listed before me for a Directions hearing, at which time I made Orders, inter alia:
(a) Requiring the wife to file and serve an Amended Response to Initiating Application together with an affidavit in support to incorporate financial issues by no later than 4.00pm on 26 May 2025. I also directed the wife to file a response to the paternal grandmother’s Application in a Proceeding by the same date and time. The wife did not comply with my orders for the filing of material instead filed her material three (3) days late;

(b) Requiring the wife to submit to hair testing for the detection of illegal and/or unprescribed substances within seven days. This order was in circumstances where the wife had not complied with an Order in the same terms made by a Judicial Registrar on 4 April 2024 (supra); and

(c) I listed the competing applications for a final hearing on 18 August 2025.

On 16 June 2025, and following a contested interim hearing, I made orders, which joined the paternal grandmother to the proceedings as the second respondent. I also made orders placing the child in the care of the paternal grandmother in circumstances where my ex-tempore reasons delivered that day reflect that I was concerned about:
(a) The child’s high absenteeism rate at school across the 2024 academic year;

(b) The wife’s excessive consumption of alcohol, which was compromising her care of the child;

(c) The fact of the wife living with a person who tested positive for the presence of an illicit substance; and

(d) The wife’s non-compliance with orders made on 4 April 2024 and 5 May 2025 requiring her to submit to hair follicle testing.

My Orders dated 16 June 2025 contemplated that the child would again spend time with the wife if:
(a) The wife produced a carbohydrate-deficient transferrin test, which was not indicative of the wife abusing alcohol; and

(b) The wife produced a hair follicle test result, which was negative for the presence of illicit substances.

The wife did not produce the evidence referred to above and as a consequence, she did not spend any time with the child between 16 June 2025 and the commencement of the final hearing on 18 August 2025.
The final hearing commenced before me on 18 August 2025. Several aspects of the wife’s conduct prior to and during the final hearing concerned me. Particularly:
(a) At 7.42am on the first day of the hearing, the wife filed a Balance Sheet. Given the time at which the document was filed, the husband’s counsel did not have the opportunity to obtain any instructions in relation to the document prior to the commencement of the final hearing. The document was prepared:
(i) Without any regard to the value placed on assets and liabilities by the husband; and

(ii) In breach of Orders made by me on 5 May 2025.

In addition, and by his affidavit filed on 13 August 2025, the husband identified that the mother had not made disclosure of her bank accounts or other assets to enable the parties to be able to construct a Joint Balance Sheet;[8]

(b) The wife maintained an application for sole decision-making responsibility and an application for the child to live with her despite her failure to comply with two orders requiring her to submit to a hair follicle test and a carbohydrate deficient transferrin test. The wife did ultimately provide a sample of her hair on 20 June 2025. The sample was positive for the presence of an illicit substance.[9] The terms of the wife’s application for the child to live with her were even more remarkable given this positive result;

(c) The wife by her Amended Response to Initiating Application filed on 31 July 2025 or her counsel’s Outline of Case Document failed to identify orders in the alternative to an order for primary care despite the obvious difficulties associated with such an application (supra);

(d) On 18 August 2025, the wife by her counsel requested time to obtain instructions from the wife and to have discussions with the husband, the paternal grandmother and the Independent Children’s Lawyer. I granted that application on the basis that among other things, I wished for the wife to provide me with orders, which she might promote in the event that her primary application was not successful. These instructions ought to have been obtained well prior to the first day of the final hearing;

(e) At 12.58pm on 18 August 2025, the wife’s counsel advised me that progress had been made in relation to the competing parenting and financial applications but that “one or two items” on the Balance Sheet remained in dispute. The wife’s counsel also advised me that the wife had arrived at the point of “constructing an offer” with respect to the competing applications. Again, such an offer ought to have been constructed well prior to the first day of the final hearing;

(f) At 4.22pm on 18 August 2025, the wife’s counsel advised me that there was “considerable agreement” between the parties but equally, the competing applications had not resolved. I was not provided with any indication whatsoever about the topics, which were agreed or the topics that remained in dispute;

(g) On the morning of 19 August 2025, I was advised by the wife’s counsel that there were still “issues” with the Balance Sheet despite having been afforded the entirety of the first day of trial to resolve the same. Further, the wife failed, despite my request, to provide me with a minute of order with respect to an alternate parenting application. The wife’s failure occurred in the face of the paternal grandmother and the Independent Children’s Lawyer being able to provide me with a proposed order agreed between them;[10]

(h) At 10.24am on 19 August 2025, I commenced hearing objections to evidence. The wife’s counsel was ill-prepared to address me causing a temporary adjournment;

(i) At about 11.16am on 19 August 2025, the wife through her counsel provided me with a proposed order in relation to the parenting applications. Although I marked the proposed order as an exhibit, I later removed it from the Court file in circumstances where the wife’s counsel advised me that I had received a document which was not drafted in accordance with her client’s instructions. At the very least, it was plain to me that there was a disconnect between the document, which had been tendered, and the instructions held by the wife’s counsel;

(j) At 12.49pm on 19 August 2025, the wife’s counsel advised me that the wife wished to amend her applications;

(k) As a consequence of the wife’s failure to properly prepare her case as described above, cross-examination of the wife did not commence until 12.53pm on the second day of the final hearing;

(l) At no stage during the final hearing did the wife’s counsel provide me with a Joint Balance Sheet, which identified whether the values placed on assets and liabilities by the husband was agreed or, if not, the value ascribed to those items. I consider this to be a significant failure on the part of the wife and her advisors in circumstances where the net assets available for distribution were modest. The wife’s failure occurred despite the listing of the applications before me on six occasions between 18 August 2025 and 27 October 2025; and

(m) Given the failure of the wife to provide me with a Joint Balance Sheet, the husband’s counsel provided me with a Balance Sheet that had been prepared by her together with handwritten amendments made by the wife’s counsel. That document was provided to me on the sixth day of the final hearing being 28 October 2025.[11] In an effort to clarify the husband’s position with respect to some items on the Balance Sheet, I sought clarification from the husband’s counsel during a hearing, which occurred on 27 November 2025.

In addition to the matters enumerated above, the wife by her conduct caused other unnecessary delays insofar as the efficient conduct of the final hearing was concerned. For example:
(a) The wife’s solicitor made efforts to obtain a valuation of the Suburb C property without sending a letter of joint instruction.[12] Rule 7.03 and Rule 7.13(3) to (5) of the Rules are clear. The husband deposed to the difficulties, which arose as a consequence of the actions of the wife and/or her advisors;[13]

(b) The wife failed to disclose all bank accounts in her name;[14] and

(c) At the conclusion of the hearing on 21 August 2025, and in the face of the husband’s application to sell the property at Suburb C, the wife’s counsel advised me that the wife wished to retain the property or, in the alternative, wished to delay the sale of the same on the basis that over the following months, the Sydney property market may rise in value. There was no evidence before me to support such an assertion. Further, the application was made in circumstances where:

(i) By his Amended Initiating application filed on 2 May 2025, the husband promoted orders on a final basis for the sale of the Suburb C property; and

(ii) The wife, by her Amended Response to Final Orders filed on 31 July 2025, supported that application.

According to the terms of her Financial Statement filed on 28 May 2025, the wife’s sole source of income was a Commonwealth benefit. She produced no information whatsoever which might have enabled me to determine whether the wife had capacity to retain the property and thereafter to pay to the husband his just and equitable entitlement. Simply, I considered the application to be misconceived and/or an application brought for tactical reasons and/or for some other nefarious purpose. Accordingly, and on 25 August 2025, I made orders requiring the parties to do all acts and things and sign all necessary documents to effect a sale of the property.

The competing parenting and financial applications were again listed on a part-heard basis on 27 October 2025 and 28 October 2025. For the reasons identified above, I consider that the husband, the paternal grandmother and the Independent Children’s Lawyer would not have been put to the expense associated with their respective attendances on these additional days if:
(a) The wife and her advisors had been properly prepared for the final hearing; and

(b) The wife had not prosecuted arguments, which were without merit (supra).

The wife failed to attend at Court on 27 October 2025 without explanation or apology to the other parties to the proceedings, the Independent Children’s Lawyer or the Court. This being so, and pursuant to r 10.27 of the Rules, I made an order that the competing applications proceed on an undefended basis.
On the afternoon of 27 October 2025 and having listened to the submissions of counsel for the husband, counsel for the paternal grandmother and counsel for the Independent Children’s Lawyer, I made orders, which had been agreed between them. Among other things, the orders provided that:
(a) The husband have sole decision-making authority in relation to all major long-term issues for the child save that until 31 December 2027 the husband and the paternal grandmother jointly exercise such responsibility;

(b) The child live with the husband conditional on the husband residing with the paternal grandmother until 31 December 2027; and

(c) The child spend no time with the wife.

On 28 October 2025, the wife again failed to attend at Court even though at one juncture, her solicitor appeared in the Court room. She did so in circumstances where I had otherwise granted her leave to withdraw and directed that the competing financial applications proceed on an undefended basis (supra). Whatever the scenario, I heard Closing Submissions delivered by the husband’s counsel and I reserved my judgment. I delivered that judgment on 3 December 2025.
The cost applications brought by the husband and the paternal grandmother have been similarly tortuous. The applications were listed for an interim defended hearing on 16 February 2026. The wife did in fact attend on that occasion. She had not, however, filed a response to the applications.
Despite the opposition of the husband and the paternal grandmother, and by Orders made on 16 February 2026, I afforded the wife an opportunity to file documents responding to the applications such response to be filed by 4.00pm on 23 March 2026. I also afforded the wife an opportunity to file an Outline of Case Document. The wife has failed to do so.
It is against this background that the cost applications filed by the husband and the paternal grandmother fall for determination.

EVIDENCE RELIED UPON

The husband relied on:
(a) An Application in a Proceeding filed on 2 January 2026;

(b) Affidavit in the father’s name filed on 2 January 2026 and 16 February 2026; and

(c) Some Tendered documents.[15]

The paternal grandmother relied on:
(a) An Amended Application in a Proceeding filed on 15 January 2026;

(b) An Affidavit filed on 13 January 2026;

(c) One tendered document;[16] and

(d) An Outline of Case Document filed on 13 January 2026.

In addition to the tendered documents, I dispensed with rule 8.15(3)(e) of the Rules such that the husband and the paternal grandmother were permitted to rely upon documents annexed to their affidavits.

LEGAL PRINCIPLES

Section 114UB(1) of the Act abolishes for the purposes of family law proceedings, the general rule that in civil proceedings, costs follow the event. It provides that each party should bear his or her own costs in such proceedings.
However, the Court retains a discretion to make an order as to costs. The relevant principles are well settled.[17] The Court has a wide discretion, which is to be exercised judicially.
Relevantly, section 114UB(2) of the Act reads as follows:
If, in proceedings under this Act, the court is of the opinion that there are circumstances that justify it in doing so, the court may, subject to subsection (3) of this section, sections 114UC and 114UD and the applicable rules of Court, make such order as to costs and security for costs, whether by way of interlocutory order or otherwise.
When considering what order (if any) is just, the legislation mandates that the Court have regard to those matters set out in s 114UB(3) of the Act. The legislation does not identify that any of those seven matters is determinative. However, the Court must consider each matter in the seven alphabetical subsections.[18] There is nothing to prevent any factor being the sole foundation for any order for costs being made.[19]
Section 114UB(3) of the Act provides that when considering what costs order (if any) should be made, the Court shall have regard to:
(a) the financial circumstances of each of the parties to the proceedings;
(b) whether any party to the proceedings is in receipt of assistance by way of legal aid and, if so, the terms of the grant of that assistance to that party;

(c) the conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings including, without limiting paragraphs (a) and (b), the conduct of the parties in relation to pleadings, particulars, discovery, inspection, directions to answer questions, admissions of facts, production of documents and similar matters, and in relation to their duty of disclosure under subsection 71B(1), 90RI(1) or 90YJA(1);

(d) whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the court;

(e) whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings;

(f) whether either party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer; and

(g) such other matters as the court considers relevant.
The manner in which costs are to be assessed is dealt with in r 12.17(1) of the Rules, which provides:
(1) The court may order that a party is entitled to costs:
(a) of a specific amount; or

(b) as assessed on a particular basis (for example, party and party, solicitor and client or indemnity); or

(c) to be calculated in accordance with the method stated in the order; or

(d) for part of the proceeding, or part of an amount, assessed in accordance with Schedule 3.

Rule 12.17(3) of the Rules sets out matters that may be considered in the determination of the quantum of costs, providing that:
(3) In making an order under subrule (1), the court may consider the following:
(a) the importance, complexity or difficulty of the issues;

(b) the reasonableness of each party's behaviour in the proceeding including by having regard to the matters set out in subrule 12.08(2);

(c) the rates ordinarily payable to lawyers in comparable proceedings;

(d) whether a lawyer's conduct has been improper, unfair, unreasonable or disproportionate;

(e) the time properly spent on the proceedings, or in complying with pre-action procedures;

(f) whether expenses (paid or payable) are fair, reasonable and proportionate.

The provisions of the Act and the Rules confer a broad discretion on a judge hearing and determining a costs application.

CONSIDERATION – HUSBAND’S APPLICATION

It is now appropriate to consider whether there are any circumstances which justify a departure from the general rule provided by s 114UB(1) by virtue of any of the considerations specified in s 114UB(3) of the Act.

The financial circumstances of each of the parties to the proceedings

The financial circumstances of the parties were outlined at length in my judgment dated 3 December 2025 with respect to the parties’ competing financial applications, namely, Arrigucci & Franjic (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 877.
The husband works as a professional manager.[20] He earns a sum of about $99,996 gross per annum.[21] The wife is unemployed.
The effect of the orders made by me on 3 December 2025 is that once they are implemented, and after some adjustments provided for therein and prior to any order as to costs:
(a) The husband on the sale of the Suburb C property will receive a cash payment of between $254,742 and $312,242. The precise sum to be received by him will be determined by the sale price achieved for the property; and

(b) The wife will receive a cash payment of between $232,761 and $275,261. Similarly, the precise sum to be received by the wife will be determined by the sale price achieved for the property.

The husband enjoys greater superannuation than the wife, however, I took this into account when arriving at a just and equitable division of the parties’ assets.
In relation to legal fees, the husband has been billed a sum of $331,737.48 to date with a further sum of $7,199.50 remaining unbilled and unpaid.[22] The husband says that he has borrowed monies from third parties including his father to satisfy his legal fees.[23] This topic is traversed at paragraphs 66 to 68 of my final judgment with respect to the parties’ competing financial applications.
For her part, and as at 19 August 2025, the wife was indebted to her solicitor by a sum of $33,500 although the wife’s Costs’ Notice indicates that by the conclusion of a five-day trial, she would have been indebted to her solicitor by a sum of about $51,500.[24] The wife’s fees are substantially less than the husband’s fees in circumstances where the wife was not represented by a legal practitioner during the period 20 January 2025 and 13 May 2025 and again, subsequent to 4 February 2026.
It follows that on the sale of the Suburb C property, each of the husband and the wife will struggle with straitened circumstances. However, it is well established that straitened circumstances and even impecuniosity are not a bar to the making of a costs order where the circumstances otherwise justify it.[25]

The conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings

This is a significant factor in the context of this matter. The relevant conduct is the party’s conduct as a litigant.[26]
I refer to the conduct of the wife during the proceedings as summarised above. Importantly, I agree with submissions made by the husband to the effect that the wife has engaged in inappropriate conduct, including a breach of Orders of the Court, failure to attend several hearings when required to do so, failure to attend on the final two days of the final hearing and the taking of obstructive and unmeritorious positions. Particularly:
(a) The wife failed to adequately prepare her case as summarised above causing significant delays on the first and second days of the final hearing;

(b) The wife breached orders made by me on 25 August 2025 requiring the parties to sell the Suburb C property. As a consequence, and on 3 December 2025, I took the step of appointing the husband as trustee for the sale of the property;

(c) The wife’s opposition on the fifth day of the final hearing to the husband’s application to sell the Suburb C property was for the reasons described above without merit and contrary to her own application;

(d) The wife’s application for the child to live with her was unmeritorious in the face of her breach of two orders over two years requiring her to submit to a hair follicle test for the detection of illegal substances. Ultimately, the wife did produce a hair follicle test result, which was positive for an illicit substance. Again, I find it difficult to understand why the wife considered it appropriate to maintain her application in the face of the positive result;

(e) The wife’s failure to attend at Court on 27 and 28 October 2025 delayed the completion of the final hearing and created argument, which ought to have been unnecessary. Her failure to attend also caused the husband difficulties because on an undefended basis, his counsel was obliged to make submissions as to the composition of the Balance Sheet in circumstances where the wife failed to comply with orders first made in May 2025 (supra). Further, the failure of the wife and/or her advisors to produce a crystallised Balance Sheet at any stage during the first six days of the final hearing was unacceptable; and

(f) The wife’s abandonment of her case and her failure or refusal to participate in the final two days of the hearing occurred after the husband had been required to endure and fund two years of litigation and no less than seven days of trial (in addition to the delivery of an interim judgment on 25 August 2025). Despite this, the wife failed to agree to any order whatsoever thereby requiring the husband to continue to prosecute his application.

I am also concerned that the parenting orders sought by the wife were not properly considered by her. For example, the wife promoted an order for no time between the child and her father despite:
(a) Failing to prosecute such an application at final hearing;

(b) Sending a message to the husband in May 2025 advising them that he is a “great dad” and that the parties ought to share the care of the child on a shared care basis;[27] and

(c) The wife’s oral concession at a changeover in May 2025 to the effect that the child “loves spending time with her dad”. Again, the wife expressed an opinion that the parties ought to share the care of the child on a shared care basis.[28]

Further, and whilst I accept that the wife’s allegations against the husband have not been tested, the husband deposes to the fabrication of allegations by the wife after it became clear to her that the husband and the Court would insist on her compliance with orders for drug testing.[29]
In relation to the parties’ competing financial applications and as discussed, the wife’s failure to comply with her obligations as to disclosure was so serious that at the beginning of the final hearing, the parties were unable to provide me with a Balance Sheet. Rule 6.17(a)(iii) of the Rules and numerous authorities make it clear that a party may be ordered to pay costs if he/she fails to make disclosure as required by the Rules.[30]
As Nygh J held in Jensen & Jensen:[31]
...if as a result of non-cooperation, obstructiveness or otherwise, one of the parties causes the conduct of the proceedings to be unduly prolonged or made unduly expensive to the other side, clearly an order for costs would be warranted.
This is a case where precisely that type of conduct has occurred.
The wife was wholly unsuccessfully with respect to her parenting application. Her application as expressed by her Response to Initiating Application filed on 31 July 2025 and her counsel’s Outline of Case Document filed on 15 August 2025 was to the effect that the wife have sole decision-making authority in relation to the child and that the child not spend any time with the husband.
The wife was also unsuccessful with respect to her opposition to the paternal grandmother’s application to join the proceedings and/or the making of any parenting orders in favour of the paternal grandmother. The interim defended hearing in relation to these applications occurred on 16 June 2025.
The wife was also unsuccessful with respect to her financial application. Particularly, and by her Amended Response to Initiating Application filed on 31 July 2025, the wife sought an order that the net proceeds of the Suburb C property be divided on a 75:25 basis in her favour. She also sought an order that the husband “transfer 75 percent of his superannuation entitlements” to the wife.[32] The effect of my Orders made on 3 December 2025 is that the parties’ non-superannuation and superannuation assets are to be divided on a 57.5 / 42.5 basis in favour of the husband.

Whether either party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer

The husband did not make any offers to settle the competing parenting and financial applications. Particularly, he says:
[The wife’s] conduct and failure to cooperate has subsequently led me to being unable to propose a reasonable settlement offer forward to her to try negotiate and propose reasonable settlement offers. I could not properly ascertain the property pool with the lack of disclosure and lack of cooperation, causing litigation to be prolonged and costs to escalate.[33]
For the reasons set out above, I accept the husband’s assertions insofar as they relate to the parties’ competing financial applications. I also accept that as a consequence of the wife’s unilateral engagement of a valuer, a valuation was obtained on which the husband could place no reliance. The wife did so despite advising a Judicial Registrar on 18 June 2025 that the parties would engage a Single Expert.[34]
The husband did not make any offer in relation to the parties’ competing parenting applications. As discussed above, criminal charges against the husband were dismissed in May or June 2024. Despite this, the wife’s application at the time of the final hearing remained that the child ought to spend no time with the husband. This being so, an offer by the husband in relation to the parties’ competing parenting applications would have been futile.

Such other matters as the Court considers relevant

I have now learned that contrary to evidence given by the wife about the location of a missing boat, she was in fact in possession of a boat owned by the husband’s father. In the alternative, the evidence suggests that the wife sold the boat to a third party, but the boat was nevertheless stored at a location where the wife was living.[35] This was despite the wife giving evidence on cross-examination that she had not seen it.
I was also provided with photographs of the Suburb C property on the wife’s departure from that property pursuant Orders made by me on 3 December 2025. Those photographs clearly identify that the property was left in a squalid condition and on their face, the photographs support a finding that the wife breached Orders first made by me on 25 August 2025 requiring the wife to keep the property in a clean and neat condition and in good order.[36]
As a consequence of the poor manner in which the wife left the property, the husband has been put to the expense of engaging and paying third parties to conduct repairs to the property to prepare it for sale. Those repairs included painting, the repair of window frames, the replacement of broken windows, the replacement of flyscreens, works to the garden and the removal of rubbish.[37] The costs associated with the works were in excess of $4,300 in circumstances where:
(a) The husband had not lived at the Suburb C property since January 2023 although he had been making all mortgage payments; and

(b) The wife breached orders made by me on 25 August 2025.

On balance, and particularly from 26 March 2025 when Justice Riethmuller indicated that a warrant may issue for the wife’s arrest due to her failure to participate in the proceedings, the litigation has been plagued by the wife’s failure and/or refusal to comply with extant orders and/or the wife’s unreasonable opposition to reasonable applications made by the husband and the paternal grandmother.
For the reasons described above, the wife’s attitude and behaviour towards the husband and the paternal grandmother were deeply problematic particularly given that the wife abandoned the proceedings after considerable time and monies had been expended. Further, I do not consider that the wife made efforts to quickly, inexpensively and efficiently bring an end to the litigation.
Section 95 of the Act is in the following terms:
(1) The overarching purpose of the family law practice and procedure provisions is to facilitate the just resolution of disputes:
(a) in a way that ensures the safety of families and children; and
(b) in relation to proceedings under this Act in which the best interests of the child are the paramount consideration – in a way that promotes the best interests of the child; and

(c) according to law; and

(d) as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible.
(2) Without limiting subsection (1), the overarching purpose includes the following objectives in relation to proceedings under this Act:
(a) the just determination of all such proceedings;
(b) the efficient use of the judicial and administrative resources available for the purposes of courts exercising jurisdiction in such proceedings;

(c) the efficient disposal of the overall caseload of courts exercising jurisdiction in such proceedings;

(d) the disposal of all such proceedings in a timely manner;
(e) the resolution of disputes at a cost that is proportionate to the importance and complexity of the matters in dispute.
Section 96(1) of the Act, in the same manner as s 68 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) imposes a duty on litigants in this Court to conduct their litigation consistently with the overarching purpose. Section 96 of the Act is in the following terms:
96 Duty to act consistently with the overarching purpose

Duty of parties
(1) The parties to proceedings under this Act must conduct the proceedings (including negotiations for settlement of the dispute to which the proceedings relate) in a way that is consistent with the overarching purpose of the family law practice and procedure provisions.
If a party is conducting proceedings consistently with the overarching purpose of the Act, then he or she must be assiduous and careful with respect to the conduct of every hearing.
The requirements of efficacy, timeliness, cost and proportionality apply to every aspect of litigation in this court. Litigants must make efforts to rationally and carefully present his or her case and avoid unnecessary arguments.
For the reasons identified above, the wife failed to conduct her case in a manner, which was consistent with the overarching purpose. Further, the wife acted in a manner, which precluded the efficient disposal of the overall caseload of the court.

CONCLUSION AS TO THE HUSBAND’S APPLICATION FOR COSTS

Having regard to the above considerations, I am satisfied that the circumstances justify an order for costs in favour of the husband.
It is the responsibility of the Court to balance the various matters set out in s 114UB(3) of the Act to arrive at a result, which it considers just.
The husband by his application seeks his costs on an indemnity basis.
The Full Court has repeatedly emphasised that an indemnity costs order should only be made in “exceptional” cases.[38] As was said by Murphy J in Prantage & Prantage [2013] FamCAFC 105; (2013) FLC 93-544 at [152]:
Indemnity costs are confined to “an exceedingly rare situation” ... in jurisdictions where “the usual rule” is that a successful party receives an order for costs (i.e. “costs follow the event”). They might, then, be seen to be more so in this jurisdiction where the “usual rule” is that “each party ... shall bear his or her own costs (s 117(1)).
Examples of the nature of the conduct required to support an indemnity costs order are set out in the commonly quoted passage from Colgate Palmolive Co and Another v Cussons Pty Ltd [1993] FCA 536; (1993) 118 ALR 248 at 256 and 257:
... it is useful to note some of the circumstances which have been thought to warrant the exercise of the discretion. I instance the making of allegations of fraud knowing them to be false and the making of irrelevant allegations of fraud... evidence of particular misconduct that causes loss of time to the court and to other parties... or in wilful disregard of known facts or clearly established law... the making of allegations which ought never to have been made or the undue prolongation of a case by groundless contentions... an imprudent refusal of an offer to compromise...
The capacity of an applicant for costs to establish the existence of one of the matters in this list will not of necessity require the making of an order for indemnity costs but may be persuasive when viewed in light of the factors set out in s 117(2A) of the Act.[39]
For the reasons described above, I am persuaded that an order for indemnity costs is warranted. Simply, costs on a party and party basis would be an inappropriate measure of compensation. When arriving at this conclusion, I bring to account the wife’s inappropriate conduct, including a breach of Orders of the Court, failure to attend several hearings when required to do so, failure to attend on the final two days of the final hearing and the taking of obstructive and unmeritorious positions. Her conduct caused the husband expense and delay.
By his affidavit filed in support of his application, the husband advised me that his costs are in excess of $300,000.[40] A Schedule of Fees tendered by the husband identifies that:
(a) The husband paid counsel fees of $58,085 such fees not including counsel’s attendance at the hearing on 16 February 2026;

(b) The husband paid his solicitors a sum of $341,958.[41] In respect of that amount, a sum of $312,530 was incurred from 15 January 2025 noting that the husband signed a Costs Agreement on 18 January 2025.[42]

Rule 12.13(4) of the Rules requires a party applying for an order for costs on an indemnity basis to inform the Court if the party is bound by a costs agreement in relation to those costs and, if so, the terms of the costs agreement.
The Costs Agreement executed by the husband on 18 January 2025[43] identifies that the firm of solicitors engaged by him would charge at the following hourly rates:
Principal
	
$595 plus GST

Solicitor
	
$495 plus GST

Paralegal
	
$350 plus GST

Law Clerk
	
$250 plus GST
The hourly rates charged by the husband’s firm of solicitors is above the costs allowable for lawyer’s work done and services performed as identified by Schedule 3 to the Rules. Despite being afforded an opportunity to do so, the wife has not made any submissions directed to the rates ordinarily payable to lawyers in comparable proceedings.
In Tekla & Tekla [2025] FedCFamC1A 245, Justice Riethmuller provided a helpful summary of the principles, which are applicable to a determination of an application for indemnity costs. Particularly, His Honour said as follows:
[59] Indemnity costs, as defined in the Rules, are not “solicitor and own client” costs; that is, indemnity costs are not simply the amount paid by the client pursuant to their costs agreement with their own solicitor because the Rules impose a test of reasonableness. As the Full Court said in Sfakianakis at 423 [36]:
... An indemnity costs order is not a blank cheque permitting the successful party to pay his or her lawyers the amount they, or the lawyers, see fit.
[60] The difference between “solicitor and client” costs and “indemnity” costs (both being based upon reasonableness) is nuanced. Rule 12.47(1) provides that a registrar “must not allow costs that ... are not fair and reasonable”, and r 12.47(2) provides for a registrar to “allow all costs reasonably incurred” when assessing indemnity costs. The difference in wording is best understood as reflecting a different onus of proof on an assessment. When “solicitor and client” costs are ordered, the onus of persuading the court or registrar that a rate or item is “reasonable” is upon the person seeking costs; in the case of “indemnity” costs, the person challenging the costs must persuade the court or registrar that the rate or item is not “reasonable”. The effect of the difference in onus will commonly result in greater amounts being allowed on an “indemnity basis”. I note, however, that the court or registrar assessing the costs may be persuaded that the amounts claimed are not reasonable, based upon a comparison with the court scale, the other party’s costs or their experience with respect to costs.
I have reviewed invoices from the husband’s solicitor annexed to his affidavit filed on 2 January 2026. On their face, the costs incurred by the husband appear to be fair and reasonable. However, my ability to assess the husband’s costs has been made difficult by the husband’s failure to prepare a short form bill of costs or an itemised schedule of costs.
The husband invited me to fix his costs in a lump sum. Ordinarily, I would be disinclined to accede to such an approach. However, the protracted and unnecessary litigation to date causes me to conclude that such an approach is warranted.
When assessing the husband’s costs, I note that as at 19 August 2025, the wife was indebted to her solicitor by a sum of $33,500 although the wife’s Costs’ Notice indicates that by the conclusion of a five-day trial, she would have been indebted to her solicitor by a sum of about $51,500.[44] As discussed above, the wife’s fees are substantially less than the husband’s fees in circumstances where:
(a) Between the inception of the proceedings and 20 January 2025,[45] the wife was represented by the Western Sydney Community Legal Centre. That service did not charge the wife for her legal representation; and

(b) The wife was not represented by a legal practitioner during the period 20 January 2025 and 13 May 2025 and again, subsequent to 4 February 2026.

The sum of $51,500 equates to costs and disbursements of $10,300 per month over the period 13 May 2025 to 28 October 2025.
Since 15 January 2025, the husband has incurred a sum of $312,530 or about $26,000 per month. In addition, he has incurred counsel fees of $56,100- and the husband has incurred:
(a) The fees associated with supervision of his time;

(b) Fees charged by the Single Expert in the parenting proceedings; and

(c) Fees charged for alcohol and drug testing.[46]

The fees described above equate to an additional $16,213. It follows that the proceedings have been financially ruinous for the husband.
On the information available, and doing the best I can with the information which has been presented, I will allow the husband slightly more than one-third of the costs incurred by him since January 2025. Those costs equate to a sum of $130,000 or about $10,000 per month over the relevant period. Such an outcome is on par with the costs charged by the wife’s legal representatives to her. For this reason, I am confident that my assessment is “logical, fair and reasonable” (Stoian v Fiening (Costs) [2014] FamCA 944; Idoport Pty Ltd v National Australia Bank Ltd [2007] NSWSC 23).

CONSIDERATION – PATERNAL GRANDMOTHER’S APPLICATION

The financial circumstances of each of the parties to the proceedings

The financial circumstances of the wife were outlined at length in my judgment with respect to the proceedings between the wife and the husband, namely, Arrigucci & Franjic (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 877.
The paternal grandmother is aged 68 years. She resigned from her employment as a Community Worker as a consequence of orders made on 16 June 2025 placing the child into her care.[47] Albeit with some assistance from the husband, the paternal grandmother has met all costs associated with raising the child.[48] The wife has made no contribution whatsoever.
As discussed above, the effect of the orders made by me on 3 December 2025 is that the wife will receive a cash payment of between $232,761 and $275,261. The precise amount will be known once the property is sold.
The legal fees and disbursements incurred by the paternal grandmother since 13 May 2025 are in the order of $77,559.[49]
For her part, and as at 19 August 2025, the wife was indebted to her solicitor by a sum of $33,500 although the wife’s Costs’ Notice indicates that by the conclusion of a five-day trial, she would have been indebted to her solicitor by a sum of about $51,500.[50]

The conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings

This is a significant factor in the context of this matter. The relevant conduct is the party’s conduct as a litigant.[51]
Before considering the conduct of each of the paternal grandmother and the wife, I note the following:
(a) The paternal grandmother’s involvement in the proceedings began in about July 2024 at which time, the Single Expert recommended that absent any time between the child and the father, the child ought to be spending time with the paternal grandparents on a regular basis;[52]

(b) On 13 December 2024, and with the consent of the parties and the Independent Children’s Lawyer, the Court made orders to the effect that as and from Friday 7 March 2025, the child spend time with the husband supervised by the paternal grandparents on each alternate Friday. The wife did not comply with that Order despite the wife’s consent having been obtained;[53]

(c) On 28 April 2025, the paternal grandmother filed an application to join the proceedings having been invited to do so by the Independent Children’s Lawyer;[54]

(d) On 28 May 2025, the wife opposed the paternal grandmother’s joinder to the proceedings arguing that the child’s “safety and wellbeing would be seriously compromised” if the child was placed in the care of the paternal grandmother or otherwise spend unsupervised time with her.[55] By an ex tempore judgment, which I delivered on 16 June 2025, I concluded that the concerns raised by the mother about the paternal grandmother were vague and without evidentiary foundation. I also recorded that I shared the paternal grandmother’s incredulity in relation to the wife’s allegations against her in circumstances where the paternal grandmother deposed to having had a very significant involvement in the child’s life with the consent of the wife at all times until the separation of the child’s mother and father; and

(e) On 16 June 2025, I made an order placing the child in the care of the paternal grandmother.

I otherwise refer to and repeat the conduct of the wife during the proceedings as summarised above. Importantly, I agree with submissions made by the paternal grandmother to the effect that the wife has engaged in inappropriate conduct, including a breach of Orders of the Court, failure to attend several hearings when required to do so, failure to attend on the final two days of the final hearing and the taking of obstructive and unmeritorious positions. Particularly:
(a) The wife failed to adequately prepare her case as summarised above causing significant delays on the first and second days of the final hearing. The paternal grandmother asserts that the “wasted day and a half of the hearing” on 18 and 19 August 2025 “cost me almost $15,000”;[56]

(b) The wife’s application for the child to live with her was unmeritorious in the face of her breach of two orders over two years requiring her to submit to a hair follicle test for the detection of illegal substances. Ultimately, and as discussed above, the wife did produce a hair follicle test result, which was positive for an illicit substance;

(c) On 25 August 2025, the wife agreed to the terms of orders that among other things:

(i) The paternal grandmother have sole decision-making authority in relation to all major long-term issues in relation to the child; and

(ii) The child live with the paternal grandmother.

The wife’s consent to these orders was surprising giving her steadfast opposition to the joinder of the paternal grandmother to the proceedings and her assertion (supra) that the paternal grandmother represented a risk to the child;

(d) The wife’s failure to attend at Court on 27 and 28 October 2025 delayed the completion of the final hearing and created argument, which ought to have been unnecessary; and

(e) The wife’s abandonment of her case and her failure or refusal to participate in the final two days of the hearing occurred after the paternal grandmother had been required to endure and fund six months of litigation.

As Nygh J held in Jensen & Jensen:[57]
...if as a result of non-cooperation, obstructiveness or otherwise, one of the parties causes the conduct of the proceedings to be unduly prolonged or made unduly expensive to the other side, clearly an order for costs would be warranted.
This is a case where precisely that type of conduct has occurred.
The wife was wholly unsuccessfully with respect to her parenting application. Her application as expressed by her Response to Initiating Application filed on 31 July 2025 and her counsel’s Outline of Case Document filed on 15 August 2025 was to the effect that the wife have sole decision-making authority in relation to the child and that the child not spend any time with the husband and daytime periods only with the paternal grandmother.

Whether either party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer

The paternal grandmother did not make any offers to settle the competing parenting applications. However, and given the wife’s conduct as described by this Judgment, I consider that the making of any offer would have been a futile exercise.

Such other matters as the Court considers relevant

From the time that the paternal grandmother became involved in these proceedings, the litigation has been plagued by the wife’s failure and/or refusal to comply with extant orders and/or the wife’s unreasonable opposition to reasonable applications made by the husband and the paternal grandmother.
For the reasons described above, the wife’s attitude and behaviour towards the husband and the paternal grandmother were deeply problematic particularly given that the wife abandoned the proceedings after considerable time and monies had been expended. Further, I do not consider that the wife made efforts to quickly, inexpensively and efficiently bring an end to the litigation.
Section 95 of the Act is in the following terms:
(2) The overarching purpose of the family law practice and procedure provisions is to facilitate the just resolution of disputes:
(a) in a way that ensures the safety of families and children; and
(b) in relation to proceedings under this Act in which the best interests of the child are the paramount consideration – in a way that promotes the best interests of the child; and

(c) according to law; and

(d) as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible.
(2) Without limiting subsection (1), the overarching purpose includes the following objectives in relation to proceedings under this Act:
(a) the just determination of all such proceedings;
(b) the efficient use of the judicial and administrative resources available for the purposes of courts exercising jurisdiction in such proceedings;

(c) the efficient disposal of the overall caseload of courts exercising jurisdiction in such proceedings;

(d) the disposal of all such proceedings in a timely manner;
(e) the resolution of disputes at a cost that is proportionate to the importance and complexity of the matters in dispute.
Section 96(1) of the Act, in the same manner as s 68 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) imposes a duty on litigants in this Court to conduct their litigation consistently with the overarching purpose. Section 96 of the Act is in the following terms:
96 Duty to act consistently with the overarching purpose

Duty of parties
(2) The parties to proceedings under this Act must conduct the proceedings (including negotiations for settlement of the dispute to which the proceedings relate) in a way that is consistent with the overarching purpose of the family law practice and procedure provisions.
If a party is conducting proceedings consistently with the overarching purpose of the Act, then he or she must be assiduous and careful with respect to the conduct of each and every hearing.
The requirements of efficacy, timeliness, cost and proportionality apply to every aspect of litigation in this court. Litigants must make efforts to rationally and carefully present his or her case and avoid unnecessary arguments.
For the reasons identified above, the wife failed to conduct her case in a manner, which was consistent with the overarching purpose. Further, the wife acted in a manner, which precluded the efficient disposal of the overall caseload of the court.

CONCLUSION AS TO THE PATERNAL GRANDMOTHER’S APPLICATION FOR COSTS

Having regard to the above considerations, I am satisfied that the circumstances justify an order for costs in favour of the paternal grandmother.
It is the responsibility of the Court to balance the various matters set out in s 114UB(3) of the Act to arrive at a result, which it considers just.
By her Amended Application in a Proceeding filed on 15 January 2026, the paternal grandmother seeks costs of and incidental to the hearing on 16 June 2025 and the costs of and incidental to the final hearing on an indemnity basis.
For the reasons described above, I am persuaded that an order for indemnity costs is warranted. Simply, costs on a party and party basis would be an inappropriate measure of compensation. When arriving at this conclusion, I bring to account the wife’s inappropriate conduct, including a breach of Orders of the Court, failure to attend several hearings when required to do so, failure to attend on the final two days of the final hearing and the taking of obstructive and unmeritorious positions. Her conduct caused the paternal grandmother expense and delay.
As discussed above, the legal fees and disbursements incurred by the paternal grandmother since 13 May 2025 are in the order of $77,559.[58]
For her part, and as at 19 August 2025, the wife was indebted to her solicitor by a sum of $33,500 although the wife’s Costs’ Notice indicates that by the conclusion of a five-day trial, she would have been indebted to her solicitor by a sum of about $51,500.[59]
I have reviewed invoices rendered by the paternal grandmother’s solicitor since May 2025 as well as the Costs Agreement provided to the paternal grandmother.[60] On their face, the costs incurred by the paternal grandmother appear to be fair and reasonable. However, and once again, my ability to assess the paternal grandmother’s costs has been made difficult by her failure to prepare a short form bill of costs or an itemised schedule of costs.
I am concerned about the financial and emotional consequences of this litigation, which for the paternal family has been ongoing since 11 December 2023. In circumstances where the costs incurred by the mother and the paternal grandmother since May 2025 are similar, I will fix the costs of the paternal grandmother in a sum of $65,000 to avoid the need for the paternal grandmother to obtain a formal assessment. I am confident that such an appropriate is “logical, fair and reasonable” having regard to the fees incurred by the wife over a similar period.[61]
